Actually, wait. I notice that the boxes swap colors when I click the yellow elements. The left box has a blue border and the right box has a gray border. When I click the yellow elements, the borders swap. This is a key mechanic.
Maybe the goal is to have the boxes in a specific configuration and then do something else. But I've tried clicking the blue blob after swapping boxes and it didn't work.
Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Clicking the gray background in the left panel
2. Clicking the checkerboard in the right panel
3. Clicking the white area in the right panel (where bars were)
4. Clicking the dark gray square in the left panel (I think I tried this but let me verify)
5. Clicking the white dots in the boxes (just tried)
Let me check what happened with the white dot click, and if nothing changed, try clicking the gray background in the left panel.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. The level 1 completion action was MOUSE(row=55, col=36), which is in the right panel area. Looking at the current board, row 55 is near the bottom of the right panel, in the area where the bars were. But I already removed all the bars.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Let me check if the box configuration changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
# Find the box outline nodes (blue and gray borders)
blue_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 10]
gray_border_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g' and n['pixels'] > 10]
print(f"Blue nodes (b): {len(blue_nodes)}")
for n in blue_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center=({center_row}, {center_col})")

print(f"Gray nodes (g): {len(gray_border_nodes)}")
for n in gray_border_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center=({center_row}, {center_col})")

# Let me try clicking the gray background in the left panel
# The gray background is node 3 with center around (17, 10)
# Let me click somewhere in the middle of the gray area
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 17, 'col': 10}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
